It is just critically important. It's sole and only purpose. Now a lot of people follow this up. They want to get the brand name into the headline. They want to get their company name into the headline. They want to communicate their offer in the headline. Not that any or all of those things can't or shouldn't be done, as long as they further the main purpose of the headline, which is to get the person to read the first sentence. That's the headline's job, is to keep them from turning the page and moving on or throwing your mail piece out and get them to read the next sentence. You've got to ask yourself, when you have a headline, does it do that? It doesn't do that, regardless of what else it does. It's wrong. I think this is an interesting example. I believe you probably know it using headlines on letters. Here's a letter of this I just did a couple days ago. This is my client's attempt. This is US gold. This is his attempt at a letter. And there's no headline at all, which means that the first sentence would then become the headline, if you left the format alone. The first sentence is, here is a once in a lifetime offer for you. Well, it's not great because why? It's not believable. It's not believable. It's nonspecific. It's interchangeable with five million different people who might also mail to this same customer that day. You know, it just doesn't get the job done because it's so generic. So the headline becomes... An urgent message about an incredible new, almost too good to be true opportunity direct to you from the Pearl Farms in the South Seas. That's a no-brainer. Of course, it's infinitely better. And it's going to people who deal with this, by the way. These are already customers who have bought gold to resell and bought stuff. So now, U.S. gold's all letterhead. Okay, I know who they are. I buy from them all the time. Opportunity has to be in there somewhere because that's really what they buy all the time, is a way to go make money. Oh, Pearls, that's new. That's new. South Seas, gee, that's kind of exotic. That's an interesting... Will they read the next sentence? Probably. Formulas. When in doubt, right headlines by formulas. They're on page 57 of the Older Mattel's Letterbook, which I presume the guys all got. Are some form... No? Okay, hold on. It's our test. Are a batch of formulas. Obviously not every formula on Earth, but there's enough here to get just about anybody started. We just looked at how to go through a publication and try and find the formulas and add to your catalog of formulas. I didn't make notes, but I got the tape. I mean, I'll go back through. We've got a couple formulas in there that frankly, I don't think are in my files anywhere, but are pretty good formulas that we found this morning. International... Exactly, exactly. The first one in my book is the classic, just a formula from the classic, they last by ten, not the piano, but now when I start to... You know, they didn't think I could do whatever, but then I did. That's formulaic. They didn't think I could work only weekends and make $100,000 a year, but when my new catalog was over, I mean, that's formulaic. And so what you want to do to make your life easy is build up a bank of filling the blank formulas that you know are consistent winners, and then you just keep using those formulas over and over and over again. This book, you guys all have this? Everybody ought to have this book. I don't know, it's called How to Write a Good Advertisement, a short course of copywriting by Victor Schwab. This is an old, old book. Melvin Powers puts it out. We stock them. They're $20 a piece. And one of the beautiful things about this book, I get it now just about every time I write a copy, is it's got a hundred of what at the time that this was published were the best headlines that you could find. They're all formulaic. And so there are literally a hundred headline formulas in this book. The three ones. Pins that look lovely to you in 24 hours or your money back. I don't know. Yeah, I noticed a check in your library. Yeah, I mean, your car engine looks better in 24 hours. You can laugh at money if you follow this simple plan. You can laugh at blank if you have a problem. Why some people almost always make money in the stock market? Why some people almost always tell it to the white? The curiosity factor is, why do some people always do that? When doctors feel right, this is what they do. Right? So this is really useful. So I suggest you write your formula, and frankly I believe most copywriters do. The headline test. The headline test is the headline test that you can find in a book. The headline test to know whether or not you've got one that works. The first test we've already described is will it make them read the next text? Here's the next test. Is will it stand alone? And so if you take your headline and you go to the star or the national choir and you do nothing but run it as a classified ad, there's really a classified ad is nothing but a headline and a response instruction. Right? That's what a classified ad is. So if you take your headline out of context, away from everything else, and run your headline, and then write for free information, and it adds to us the question is, would anybody write to you? And that's the way to test a headline. And we can tell if we've got some good ones or bad ones. Here's, NRI's at home training gives you the computer skills you need to succeed in today's hottest growth fields. Well, that'll work if we pull that out of there. NRI's at home training gives you the computer skills you need to succeed in today's hottest growth fields. Write for more information, box three, grant central station. If you have any interest at all in getting a better job and learn how to do the computer, that headline works. There's one. Your headline is, let's cut to the bottom line. Now, it's cutesy. This is exactly what I was talking about before. There's a picture of a scissors cutting the page in half, right? And then all the copy talks about getting through all the confusion of all the other opportunity ads, and let's get to the bottom line. But if you take it away from all that, it don't work. Let's cut to the bottom line, write for more information, box three, grant central station. And that's the test. Does my headline work? Well, let's see. What are they really selling? They're selling a business opportunity in windshield repair, profit potential of 70,000 a year, and no franchise, the state-of-the-art equipment. I mean, I probably largely told the truth. You know, amazing opportunity for blue-collar folks earn up to 70,000 a year, no franchise fee. Right. I mean, now that works. I think it may not be the best thing we could do if we sat around here and worked, and worked, and worked, but it works. And I mean, so many people make this mistake. Here's an ad for a business opt to get into the cruise business, right? But here's their headline. Captain your own ship. It only works if you read everything else. Now you got it, but that's not its job. If you're going to read everything else first, you might as well drop that. And in fact, their subhead works better. Imagine selling a product that everybody wants. You'd be better off with that up there in the reverse than you would this. Because this only works after I've read this. Well, if I've already read this, what the hell do I need this year for? I've just wasted that much space. Can you see the problem? Now over here, this guy's got it. Inventor reveals how to earn 400 bucks a day with free tires. That's what your formula is. Yeah. That's the best you can do. Yep. You know, look at this headline. They're waiting for you. And all these photos. See, you've got to get down to, oh yeah, look at this space. And I mean, you've got to go read everything. I mean, they might as well have run a half-page ad for all the impact they had. See, and it's guys that just don't know. I mean, they go to an ad agency, and the ad agency takes their money. And again, coming from an advertising standpoint, if you put this ad in money, or fortune, you'd say, well, that's like an ad like your budget. For direct response, it's useless. The guy that bought Ted Nicholas's business, how to form your own corporation by phone, totally illegal in any state media, in as little as eight minutes as low as 45 bucks. That's a great headline. Great, great to me. You know, and the pre-head says legal breaks. See, this guy's got that. You could run that ad as a classified ad, and you get response. That passes the test. A lot of people want to use their headline space for their company name. These guys have done that. They've used the most valuable space in the ad, which is the headline, for their company name sculpture world. Who gives a shit? Who cares? Right? So here's the headline that we care about. Here's how you can profit by producing a hottest new poster, photographic, and display art products from your home or shop. Your company name belongs down here, where the response is not up here. You know what I'm realizing? A long time ago, I'm embarrassing the cleverness of them all. Oh, yeah. Yeah. How do you see that? I'm urged to think, well, it's a question of, am I being creative for the sake of creativity? Which is a word thinking. Or am I being creative because it advances the cause of the sale? Right? And that's the tough question. In our business, in television, that's a very costly question. Because you can add 200 grand to a TV show budget for creativity for creativity to say. It doesn't do a damn thing to make the sale. Here we'll be able to show it without sorry. Not here, it doesn't hurt you on the front end, but it hurts you on the back end. So, let's go ahead and do a headline test. Next thing on the list is subheads. Subheads are at least as important, if not as important as the headline. And all the same rules apply. The subhead is a headline. It should stand alone. It should cause them to read the next sentence. A good subhead will suck the skimmer in and either cause them to go back and start from the beginning or at least start reading from that point. Here are my subheads in this letter that I rewrote for IUS Gold for example. Now this paragraph is really a sneaky subhead because it's set in bold-faced type, the entire paragraph, and you'll research the same thing as the subhead. It says, well, I'm about to get you or your wife a fantastic pearl pendant and earrings set at an unbelievable low cost and let you make big money selling these sets. Read on because this is one unusual opportunity you will not want to miss. The next subhead, even a Hollywood starlet, would find this jewelry too beautiful to believe. Next subhead, oh, the money you can make fast with this exciting jewelry. Next subhead, how this jewelry will attract money to you like a magnet. Next subhead, for you with every year in a pendant set you purchased before March 31st, I'll add a beautiful 14 karat solid gold 18 inch chain. Each one of those works as a headline and that's what you want your subheads to do. Photo captions. Photo captions should be just like headlines and subheads. You should never have a photo caption that says, here's Alan Barbara at the kitchen table. You should have a photo caption that says, here's Alan Barbara at the kitchen table making more money per minute than most people make per hour. Because a lot of people will look at the picture and read the caption before they do anything else or instead of doing anything else we could train to do it our entire lives. So a photograph, job is to get them to read the caption and the captions job is to get them to go back and read the copy. So anytime we find an ad with a photo in it with no captions we know we got somebody that does not know what they are doing because every photo should have a caption. Then if the caption is just a factual caption then we know they don't know what's what they're doing. See here's a big waste. Here's DuraClean. Which by the way works. It's not necessarily deadly but it could work better. The headline is why left IBM is a clean car. Now we got a picture of the guy sitting in his office. Photo caption says, John Garioli left IBM to manage his own DuraClean franchise. Well it really does the same thing. So one or the other is wasted. So we should use one or the other space to communicate some other important compelling reason to read this whole ad. If we're going to use this why left IBM is a clean car. Let's say John Garioli left IBM to manage his DuraClean franchise and now makes more money in a month than most IBM executives make in a year. Oh well I don't want to feel about that. Okay. Or here's John sitting in his office with a shirt and tie on making money in the carpet cleaning business. How can that be? Right? Because one of the benefits we're going to sell is you don't ever get your hands dirty. You run the business. You hire other guys to go do the carpet. It could be a factual statement. It should be another headline. And it should pass the read. All the time. But it could be a whole other point. And it should pass all our tests of headlines. It should motivate them to read more. It should stand all. It should fit one of our formulas. It's one of the formulas showing the benefit of whatever that is. Sure. When in doubt. When in doubt go with your best benefit. Go with your best benefit. In the telephone ad the best benefit we got is that we're putting them in business for 29 bucks. What is this? That headline. What is the price? Oh it sells a $99.00 manual about how to get into long distance where you should sell in business. Yeah. You know the best thing we got there is you know how a $29.95 investment can put you into the most exciting business. You know the twist there is they had to sell some fuck. But we're not positioning it that way. We're positioning it that we're putting you in business for $29.95. Now if I was going to put a photo in there. Which I don't because I don't have any space. You know. But if I was going to you know if I was going to well here. Here's while you're active in the business you also get a free car. Okay that's what you get in a company that's one of that. That's what we could have a photo in here of a guy standing next to us. We're saving. Now what capture are we going to put under that? Here's John next to us. We're saving. That's the capture of all these people. What's another capture going to be. Here's John next to the free car that you can get to while you're active in this business. Really good. Unique selling proposition. Everybody knows what a unique selling proposition is. Damn few people go to the trouble we get one or figure one out. But it is enormously helpful if you will take the time to get a unique selling proposition. Unique selling proposition is something that differentiates you from your competition. If you're going to run an ad in the magazine it differentiates you from all the other advertisers. And it needs to be concise easily stated. The next best thing is to make it sound like you got one even if you ain't got one. You know I mean we in the Jeff ad you come back to that for a second. I mean our headlines start with a completely different approach to mail order. Well now mail orders are hot button in the afternoon. Everybody loves mail orders. So what we've tried to do here is we say we're different than every other ad you go to see if you're about the mail order business. None of the two of them matter to us. We're not. But we're going to get them reading because we stand out like we are. A dramatic selling proposition. And I know we have a unique selling proposition. We at least want a dramatic selling proposition. One of the great ones. This is subscriber of ours. I love this guy. He sells a product called the secret. Speed hitting. Speed hitting. And here's his dramatic selling proposition. Hotty hit a man 11 times or more in one second or less and exploded the action before he can blink an eye. That's a pretty dramatic selling proposition. I mean frankly I'd like to know if I'm able to do that. I don't know that I am. And by the way this is a great ad on one of the brakes. They want to look at one of his ads. I put it in an old news water. I mean he really did a job here. This is an ad that runs in black belt magazine. Obviously this is a niche market. But I mean he really works fine. Copy. These are just some of his bullet points. A simple way to get more hits in a second than you can legally handle. Five powerful methods for being faster than your opponent. One crucial thing to do that will almost guarantee your opponent can't see you move. How to really hit him and not go to jail. It's great. I'll run it at him. It's just a terrific ad. But I mean that's a dramatic selling proposition. How to hit a man 11 times or more in one second or less. He was at the conference in Vegas. He came up to me and I said, she did. I mean you don't look dangerous enough. And he said, well watch. I'll put my game face on. You know, you know his eyes. I mean the guy literally started to look a little scary. I got away with it. Opportunity gap analysis is one unique way to try and build a unique selling proposition. And what opportunity gap analysis is, that's how Tom Monahan built his unique selling proposition. It's identified what all your competitors are doing universally badly. And focus on that as your unique selling proposition. It's identified what all your competitors do badly. The one thing they all mess up well in the pizza business, it was delivered. I mean there was good pizza and bad pizza. But the one universal thing that was true about the pizza business prior to Domino's was trying to get the damn thing delivered in one piece. We did a reasonable length of time while it's still hot. Good luck. I mean most of us got pizza delivered where you had to scrape the stuff off the top of the box. Or it was cold and hard as a brick. And so Monahan focused on that. At the exclusion of other things. I mean I make the point that if you use your unique selling proposition, you know it doesn't say anything about mama's recipe from the old country. It doesn't say anything about only using the freshest ingredients. It doesn't even say it's good pizza. You know, it focuses on 30 minutes or less. That's the focus of the unique selling proposition. And he did it very deliberately based on seeing the one thing nobody else could do well. And that's one great way to go to unique selling proposition. Well, Tom doesn't have to solve that problem. But the guys at 11 Domino's do. What they landed on, which I think is a bad idea, is they're... Right now they're going with a premium driven strategy. I mean their unique selling proposition is something for nothing. And with everything you buy you get something else free. There's free bread with this one and free salad with this one and free coke with that one. You know, that's their approach to advertising. Well, I think they've got that they're facing problems with that down the road. If they don't... I mean I would have spent the money to try and solve the other problem personally. But on that one at Domino's. But setting aside their current problems, they built their entire business on the strength of a great unique selling proposition. That was very thoroughly focused on what everybody else was doing badly. I think the biggest and least understood part of copyright is that most people mess up is this issue of reform. They write the same copy for everything as opposed to creating rapport with their audience. And you will find that great direct response copy takes the time just like you would do in a sales call. You know, very rarely if you're making a personal sales call, you can walk into the guy's office or walk into John & Mary's house and go right into the sale. Hi John & Mary, let me show you the vacuum cleaner demonstration. Where's your mattress? You know, there's some foreplay, there's some warm up, there's some get to know each other. Gee, I got a dog just like yours, you know. Oh, I see you bold, you know, my brothers. I mean there's some rapport skills used in the selling process, you know, to try and create some friendliness and warmth. You are the same wavelength and I think you have to move that into your copy as well. It's very difficult to do unless you're practicing the next one, which is message to market match. And that's why niching is easier than selling to the general public when you're writing copy to a market that you really, really understand and have a lot in common with. It's much easier to create rapport than it is when you're writing to a group of people who either because they are so diverse there is no commonality or you have no natural understanding of them. And that's why I, beginners in direct response always try to get them to go back to whence they came. You know, if the guy used to be an architect, find something to sell to architects. So when you write to them, you and they are on the same wavelength. You know, now most people don't want to do that, they want to get as far away from whatever the hell they used to do as is humanly possible, you know, which just makes their job harder in my opinion at learning this. The best way to learn is to go where you already have an understanding of who you're about to sell to, and so it becomes easy to create rapport with them. I use the NSA market as the best example of this for me that I know of, and you're an NSA market. National Speakers Association, so you've got this piece, right? Yeah. Yeah. Anybody else in your senior's piece? John, you saw it at the last seminar. This is the most recent thing we did in the NSA. National Speakers Association has about 4,400 people in it, and I joined in 1978, maybe 1,500. And the turn is about a third every year. Since 1978, with only a couple campaigns a year, I've never taken less than $100,000 a year out of this marketplace, some years ago. Now that's a lot of money to get year after year after year out of that small of a pod.
